coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated organic conductive polymer for advanced ternary amorphous solar-blind UV photovoltaic photodetectors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Han
Yang, Haolin
Li, Weisen
Liang, Jiarong
Lin, Zhuogeng
Tang, Xingui
Liu, Yanghui
Zhang, Dan
Zheng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an interface engineering approach that regulates the Schottky barrier height and the interface defects concentration at the alloy compound/electrode interface is proposed to achieve high performance multi-component amorphous solar-blind ultraviolet (SBUV) photovoltaic photodetectors (PDs). The PP@GQDs, fabricated by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into a conductive polymer (PEDOT:PSS), are designed to function as both a conductive window layer and a defect passivation layer in a Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction. Compared with the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD, the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PD achieves a higher photoresponsivity (∼71.5 mA/W) and a larger external quantum efficiency (EQE) (about 34.6 %) under 256 nm irradiation with 8.6 μW optical power and at 0 V bias. Additionally, it possesses a larger SBUV–Vis rejection ratio (R256 nm/425 nm ∼ 1.9 × 103) and a higher detectivity (∼ 8.8 × 1011 Jones), which is an order of magnitude higher than that of PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD. The balance between the separation efficiency and the transport efficiency of carriers also plays a key role in the high performance of the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PDs. Furthermore, the device demonstrates potential applications in high-speed, low-power and interference-resistant SBUV optical communication systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar-blind ultraviolet
Photodetector
Amorphous
Interface engineering
Graphene quantum dots
Optical communication

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of Lu0.39 In0.61 O film synthesis and PP@GQDs/Lu0.39 In0.61 O/GaN PD fabrication process.

(a) SEM of Lu0.39 In0.61 O/GaN heterojunction. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Lu 4d, (c) In 3d, (d) C 1s in PEDOT:PSS layer and (e) C 1s in PP@GQDs layer. (f) Transmittance spectrum of Lu0.39 In0.61 O film deposited on quartz. The band gap estimation of Lu0.39 In0.61 O film is shown in the inset.

Basic optoelectronic properties of PP@GQDs/Lu0.39 In0.61 O/GaN SBUV PD. (a) Schematic diagram of the device structure. (b) I-V curves of the device in the dark and under 256 nm illumination. (c) I-T curve of the device under 0 V bias voltage and 8.6 μW SBUV illumination. (d) I-V curves of the device under SBUV illumination with different optical powers. (e) Variation of VOC and ISC with optical power. (f) Variation of the photo-to-dark current ratio (PDCR) with optical power. (g) I-T curves of the device at 0 V bias and under periodic SBUV illumination with variable power. (h) Decay time versus optical power.

Optoelectronic performance comparison between PD1 and PD2 . (a) I-V curves of the detectors in the dark. (b) I-V curves of the detectors under 256 nm illumination. (c) Variation of the R(256 nm/425 nm) of the two devices at different bias voltages. I-T curves of PD1 and PD2 at 0 bias voltage (d) and −1 V bias voltage (e).

Plots of d(V)/d(ln J) versus J in PD1 (a) and PD2 (b). (c) Schottky barrier heights between Lu0.39 In0.61 O and metals with various work functions. (d) I-V curves of the PEDOT:PSS layer and the PP@GQDs layer. (e) Diagram of the photo-excited carrier transport processes at the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39 In0.61 O interface and PP@GQDs/Lu0.39 In0.61 O interface.

Performance comparison of PD2 (VolGQDs /VolPP ∼ 40 %), PD3 (VolGQDs /VolPP ∼ 66 %), and PD4 (VolGQDs /VolPP ∼ 400 %). (a) I-V curves in the dark, and (b) I-V curves under 256 nm illumination with 8.6 μW power. (c) PDCR, (d) R256 nm , (e) SBUV–Vis (R256 nm/425 nm ) and (f) D* as a function of voltage, respectively. (g) I-T curves and decay times of the devices at 0 V bias and periodic SBUV illumination (h) Relationship between Isc and SBUV optical power variation at 0 V bias.

SBUV optical communication application of PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PD. (a) Schematic diagram of the SBUV optical communication system based on PP@GQDs/Lu0.39 In0.61 O/GaN SBUV PD. (b) Digital data waveform of Morse code encoded signals received by the photodetector.
Performance of photovoltaic type SBUV PDs based on amorphous ternary alloy materials.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated organic conductive polymer for advanced ternary amorphous solar-blind UV photovoltaic photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Sensing Physics and System Integration Applications, School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Sensing Physics and System Integration Applications
School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Sensing Physics and System Integration Applications, School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials, Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University, Sun Yat-sen University, Shenzhen 518107, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials and Technologies, School of Materials, Sun Yat-sen University, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an interface engineering approach that regulates the Schottky barrier height and the interface defects concentration at the alloy compound/electrode interface is proposed to achieve high performance multi-component amorphous solar-blind ultraviolet (SBUV) photovoltaic photodetectors (PDs). The PP@GQDs, fabricated by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into a conductive polymer (PEDOT:PSS), are designed to function as both a conductive window layer and a defect passivation layer in a Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction. Compared with the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD, the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PD achieves a higher photoresponsivity (∼71.5 mA/W) and a larger external quantum efficiency (EQE) (about 34.6 %) under 256 nm irradiation with 8.6 μW optical power and at 0 V bias. Additionally, it possesses a larger SBUV–Vis rejection ratio (R256 nm/425 nm ∼ 1.9 × 103) and a higher detectivity (∼ 8.8 × 1011 Jones), which is an order of magnitude higher than that of PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD. The balance between the separation efficiency and the transport efficiency of carriers also plays a key role in the high performance of the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PDs. Furthermore, the device demonstrates potential applications in high-speed, low-power and interference-resistant SBUV optical communication systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wide-bandgap semiconductors have exhibited significant advantages in optoelectronic devices, such as photodetectors, radio frequency devices, and microwave semiconductor devices, due to their outstanding thermal and chemical resistance, as well as high breakdown voltage [1–3]. Among these devices, solar-blind ultraviolet (SBUV) photodetectors (PDs), which are based on semiconductors with a bandgap greater than 4.9 eV, stand out for their low background noise, easy integration, and narrow response spectrum. These characteristics make them widely applicable in various fields, such as space secure communication, environmental monitoring and forest fire prevention [4,5]. At present, elemental semiconductors like diamond and binary compound semiconductors, including Ga2O3 and AlN, are common candidates for SBUV PDs [6–8]. Additionally, amorphous multi-component compounds that have a bandgap greater than 4.9 eV and are achieved through alloy engineering are also ideal candidate materials for fabricating SBUV detectors [9–11].

However, the chemical composition of amorphous compounds tends to deviate significantly from stoichiometric ratios, due to the absence of long-range order in the internal arrangement of atoms or molecules [12,13]. This lack of order leads to the formation of a large number of intrinsic defects in most of the multi-component amorphous compounds [14–16]. Typically, these defects act as trapping centers for the photogenerated carriers, resulting in most SBUV detectors based on multi-component amorphous semiconductors exhibiting low detectivity (D*) and a low SBUV–Vis rejection ratio, particularly in low-power operating mode [17,18]. Considering the unavoidable formation of a large number of intrinsic defects caused by deviations from stoichiometric ratios, employing interface engineering to reduce defect concentration on amorphous surface appears to be a reliable strategy for manufacturing multi-component amorphous SBUV PDs [19].

PEDOT:PSS (poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)) polymer, which is a p-type organic conductive polymer with rich surface dangling bonds and high SBUV transmittance, has been shown to effectively passivated surface defects and to act as an excellent transparent conductive window layer in optoelectronic devices [20,21]. However, PEDOT:PSS is composed of p-doped PEDOT with positive charge and water-soluble PSS with negative charge. Obviously, the presence of PSS chains inevitably negatively impacts its overall conductivity [22,23]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are 0-dimensional materials with high SBUV transparency and high charge mobility, as well as various functional groups. When GQDs are hybridized with PEDOT:PSS, the optical properties of PEDOT:PSS should remain unaffected and the electrical properties of PEDOT:PSS can be enhanced [24,25].

In this work, we spin-coat the GQDs-decorated organic conductive polymer (denoted as PP@GQDs) onto the Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction surface to construct a high performance amorphous SBUV PD. Under 0 V bias and 256 nm illumination, the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD demonstrates a responsivity of 71.5 mA/W, a fast response speed of 175 ms, and a large SBUV–Vis rejection ratio (R256 nm/R425 nm) of 1.9 × 103. Compared to the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN device without GQDs, the detectivity (D*) of this device has improved by one order of magnitude to 8.8 × 1011 Jones at 0 V bias, and SBUV–Vis rejection ratio has increased fourfold. The high performance of the device is attributed to the improvement in electrical properties and the enhancement of the defect passivation effect in the PP@GQDs layer. Furthermore, it has been found that the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN device achieves optimal performance when the volume fraction ratio of GQDs to PEDOT:PSS solution is 40 %. This optimal performance is attributed to the appropriate concentration of GQDs doping within the organic polymer layer, which enables a balance between carrier separation efficiency and transport efficiency.

Fig. 1 illustrates process of the Lu0.39In0.61O film synthesis and the fabrication of PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD. Initially, the ternary amorphous Lu0.39In0.61O was prepared successfully by magnetron sputtering, using In2O3 (purity 99.99 %) and Lu2O3 (purity 99.5 %) ceramic targets simultaneously. During the sputtering process, the power setting for the In2O3 target was 30 W, whereas the power setting for the Lu2O3 target was 150 W. Meanwhile, 20 sccm of Ar and 3 sccm of O2 were introduced into the reaction chamber to maintain a chamber pressure of 0.6 Pa. At room temperature, a Lu0.39In0.61O film with a bandgap of 5.2 eV was deposited on a n-GaN substrate to construct the Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction. The carrier concentration and mobility of the n-type GaN were measured as 7.7 × 1018 /cm3 and 133 cm2/(V∙s), respectively. Subsequently, the PEDOT:PSS CLEVIOS-PH 1000 solution (purchased from Xi'an Baolaite Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd.) and the GQDs solution, with an average size of approximately 15 nm (purchased from Suzhou Tanfeng Technology Company), were diluted with deionized water, respectively. The volume ratio of PEDOT:PSS to deionized water was 1:3, and the volume ratio of GQDs to deionized water is 1:9. These two diluted solutions were then mixed in a 1:1 vol ratio to obtain the PP@GQDs solution. Using spray deposition technology, the diluted PEDOT:PSS solution and the PP@GQDs solution (with 40 % volume fraction ratios of GQDs to PEDOT:PSS) were separately deposited onto the Lu0.39In0.61O film, constructing the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN and PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunctions. Finally, the fabrication process of PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV photodetectors was completed by welding metallic indium (In) onto the n-GaN substrate.

The cross-sectional morphology of the Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction is shown in Fig. 2 a, indicating that the Lu0.39In0.61O film is approximately 245 nm thick. The thickness of the film ensures sufficient SBUV absorption in the photosensitive layer. The chemical elements of the film can be identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Two core characteristic peaks corresponding to the binding energies of the Lu 4d orbitals are observed at 195.9 eV (Lu 4d5/2) and 205.6 eV (Lu 4d3/2). Additionally, the peaks for the binding energy of the In 3d orbitals are located at 444.1 eV (In 3d5/2) and 451.6 eV (In 3d3/2), as shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c. These observations confirm the presence of Lu and In elements in the film [26,27]. To verify the presence of GQDs in the PP@GQDs layer, the C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of both the PEDOT:PSS layer and the PP@GQDs layer are analyzed in detail. In Fig. 2d, the C1s spectrum of PEDOT:PSS exhibits binding energies at 284.5 eV (corresponding to the aromatic CC bonds associated with PEDOT) and 288.7 eV (corresponding to the CO/CS bonds of the PSS chain [25]). Once GQDs are embedded in the PEDOT: PSS layer, they interact strongly with both PEDOT and PSS chains, resulting in a red shift in the peaks of CC and CO/CS bonds (as shown in Fig. 2e). From another perspective, this also confirms that the PP@GQDs layer is indeed a hybrid composition of PEDOT:PSS and GQDs. Fig. 2f displays the transmission spectrum of the Lu0.39In0.61O film, which is deposited on a quartz substrate. The average transmittance of the film exceeds 80 % in both the ultraviolet (UV) and visible regions. Based on the relationship between incident photon energy (hν), absorption coefficient (α) and bandgap (Eg ) [28], we estimate the Eg of the Lu0.39In0.61O film to be approximately 5.2 eV. This suggests that the film could be utilized as a photosensitive layer of SBUV detectors.

The vertically structured PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV detector is shown in Fig. 3 a. In this configuration, GQDs are embedded in the PEDOT:PSS to form a PP@GQDs hybrid organic conductive layer. From Fig. 3b, it is observed that the photocurrent of the device exceeds the dark current by three orders of magnitude at 0 V bias and under SBUV illumination, indicating that the device possesses a good signal-to-noise ratio. In addition, the device exhibits an open-circuit voltage (VOC ) of 0.54 V, which confirms its potential for zero-power-consumption SBUV photodetection. Under 0 V bias and a single-cycle 256 nm illumination with 8.6 μW optical power, the decay time of the device is 175 ms (Fig. 3c). This suggests a fast SBUV response speed is achieved in the device. According to the definition of detectivity (D*), as given by the following formula [29]:
(1)
D ∗
=
R /
2 e
I
dark
/ S
R is the photoresponsivity, e is the elementary charge, Idark is the dark current, and S is the electrode area.

The formula listed allows us to calculate the D* of the device at 0 V bias as 8.8 × 1011 Jones. Subsequently, we provide further discussion on the device's performance at 0 V bias condition. From Fig. 3d, the photocurrent of the device increases with the increase in SBUV optical power. This increase in photocurrent is attributed to the enhanced generation of photogenerated carriers in the Lu0.39In0.61O photosensitive layer when exposed to higher optical power SBUV illumination. As the SBUV power increases from 5.9 μW to 8.6 μW, the VOC rises from 0.42 V to 0.54 V (Fig. 3e). Concurrently, the short circuit current (ISC ) will also increase with the increase in optical power [30]. The ISC as a function of optical power is fitted with an exponential function as follows [31]:
(2)
I
sc
∝ A
P n
here, A is a constant, P is the optical power, and n is the exponential factor.

The fitting factor (n = 0.8) deviates from the ideal exponential factor of 1, indicating the presence of interface defect states in the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN device. Furthermore, the PDCR of the device increases with increasing optical power (Fig. 3f). Even under weak SBUV illumination with 5.9 μW optical power, the PDCR of the device reaches 988, indicating a high signal-to-noise ratio and the ability of detecting weak SBUV signals.

From Fig. 3g, the saturated photocurrent of the device increases with increasing optical power at 0 V bias. Moreover, under periodic SBUV illumination with various optical powers, the device presents repeatable and stable I-T curves, suggesting the steadiness and reliability of the device in detecting SBUV signals. The decay time of the device varies slightly when the optical power is increased from 5.9 μW to 8.6 μW (Fig. 3h), indicating that the detector can effectively track complex SBUV signals. Furthermore, even after the device is stored for 6 months and 8 months, the variation of the photocurrent remains small under the same SBUV illumination (Fig. S1 in Supplemental Information), implying that the device possesses stable optoelectronic properties.

To analyze the effect of GQDs embedded in the PEDOT:PSS layer on the performance of the Lu0.39In0.61O SBUV PD, PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN (PD1) and PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN (PD2) are constructed at the same conditions, and a detailed performance comparison is made. Under various bias voltages, PD2 exhibits significantly smaller dark currents (Fig. 4 a), and has an order of magnitude higher in photocurrent than PD1 (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the VOC of PD2 is 1.2 times greater than that of PD1. These results suggest that PD2, with GQDs embedded in the PEDOT:PSS layer, exhibits superior SBUV photodetection performance, particularly in low-power consumption mode. By analyzing the photocurrent of these devices under 256 nm and 425 nm illumination (as shown in Fig. S2 of Supplemental Information), it is found that the R256 nm/R425 nm ratio of PD2 is higher than that of PD1 at reverse bias voltage (Fig. 4c). This indicates that PD2 possesses a stronger capability for SBUV detection.

Under 8.6 μW SBUV illumination and at a bias of 0 V, the decay time of PD2 is 175 ms longer than that of PD1 (Fig. 4d). This difference is consistent even when the SBUV optical power varies, with PD2 always exhibiting longer decay times at a bias of 0 V (Fig. S3 in Supplemental Information). This phenomenon can be attributed to the extended lifetime of photogenerated carriers, which results from the passivation of Lu0.39In0.61O interface states by PP@GQDs. At −1 V bias, PD2 exhibits a response speed more than one order of magnitude faster than PD1 (Fig. 4e), indicating that the built-in electric field strength has a greater impact on the device response speed than the carrier lifetime under the condition of applying an external bias voltage. All of these results demonstrate that the embedding of GQDs in a PEDOT:PSS layer, which serves as a conductive window layer, can significantly enhance the performance of Lu0.39In0.61O SBUV PD.

Next, the causes of the differences in the performance of PD1 and PD2 are discussed in detail. Based on the different work functions and Fermi energy levels of the materials [9], a Schottky junction is formed when PEDOT:PSS comes into contact with Lu0.39In0.61O. The interface barrier in the Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN heterojunction is not taken into account due to the large number of interface states in the ternary amorphous film. The ideal factor and Schottky barrier height in PD1 and PD2 devices are analyzed by the thermal electron emission model. When V > 3kT/q, the dark current density J of the devices satisfies the following relationship with the voltage V [32]:
(3)
V = R
A
etf
J + n
Φ B
+
nkT
/ q
ln
J /
A
∗
T 2
where R is the diode series resistance, Aetf is the effective area of the diode, ФB is the Schottky barrier height, n is the ideality factor, q is the elementary charge, T is the room temperature, k is the Boltzmann constant, and A*(=120 cm-2K-2A) is the Richardson constant.

The ideal factor values for PD1 and PD2 according to Equation (3), are 2.1 and 1.12 (as illustrated in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b). The ideal factor of PD2 with GQDs is closer to 1 than PD1 without GQDs, suggesting a lower density of interface states in PD2. Subsequently, the function H(J) is defined as follow:
(4)
H
J
=
nkT
/ q
l n
J /
A ∗
T 2

Equations (3) and (4) are used to derive a linear relation between the functions H and the J, so that the Schottky barrier heights of PD1 and PD2 can be fitted and calculated as 0.73 eV (Fig. 5a) and 1.64 eV (Fig. 5b). Both values are lower than the ideal PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O Schottky barrier height (2.81 eV) [9,33]. The reason for this can be that the interface states in the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O Schottky junction induce a Fermi level pinning effect, thereby decreasing the Schottky barrier height. To evaluate the degree of Fermi level pinning of Lu0.39In0.61O film, Ag/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN, Au/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN, and Pt/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN devices are additionally fabricated. Based on the theory of thermionic emission, the Schottky barrier heights of these nine devices are calculated and are presented in Fig. S4 (Supplemental Information). According to the definition of pinning factor S [34,35], the calculated pinning factor of 0.17 is much smaller than 1 (Fig. 5c), confirming that the Fermi level of Lu0.39In0.61O is strongly pinned. Additionally, the higher Schottky barrier height in PD2 means better carrier separation efficiency and superior photodetection performance under SBUV illumination. The Schottky barrier height of PD2 is closer to the ideal value compared to PD1, suggesting that embedding low-dimensional GQDs into the PEDOT:PSS matrix can decrease the density of interface states.

Furthermore, the PP@GQDs layer exhibits greater conductivity compared to the PEDOT:PSS layer (Fig. 5d). This can be attributed to the high mobility of the carriers in GQDs embedded in PEDOT:PSS, enhancing the conductivity of the organic conductive layer. The higher conductivity of the PP@GQDs layer implies a higher work function, further confirming that the barrier height at the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O interface is higher than the one at the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O interface. Additionally, PD2 has a lower threshold voltage under forward bias compared to PD1 (Fig. S5 in Supplemental Information), indicating fewer interface states at the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O interface, which reduces charge trapping and facilitates charge release.

Fig. 5e illustrates the transport process of photo-generated carriers at the PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O interface and PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O interface. Here, a higher interface states density is formed at PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O interface. When the photoexcited holes in Lu0.39In0.61O layer drift towards PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O interface, they are captured by these interface states, resulting in low carrier separation efficiency and short carrier lifetime in PD1. When GQDs are embedded in PEDOT:PSS/Lu0.39In0.61O interface, the density of the initial interface states will decrease, and the hole mobility will be enhanced. This will lead to higher separation efficiency and longer carrier lifetime. Consequently, the PD2 will show a higher responsivity and a higher EQE.

Furthermore, under 0 V bias voltage and 256 nm illumination with an optical power of 8.6 μW, the decay time of the devices increases with the increase in VolGQDs/VolPP of PP@GQDs layer. From Fig. 6g, PD4 with the largest VolGQDs/VolPP has the slowest decay time of 1.47 s. It indicates that the decay time of the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN device primarily depends on the carrier traversal time at 0 V bias voltage. In the PP@GQDs conductive layer with a larger VolGQDs/VolPP, the reduced mobility of photogenerated carriers results in slower response speeds. The fitting factors of Isc for devices with different VolGQDs/VolPP are shown in Fig. 6h. As the VolGQDs/VolPP increase from 40 % to 66 %, the fitting factor rises from 0.8 to 1.5 and then falls to 0.5. The reason for this is that although GQDs passivate interface defects and increase carrier separation efficiency, they also serve as scattering centers, which reduces carrier transport efficiency. It has been verified by examining the SEM morphology of the PP@GQDs conductive layer with different VolGQDs/VolPP (as shown in Fig. S7 of Supplemental Information). The conductive layer enriched with a higher concentration of GQDs exhibits a greater number of aggregates, indicating the formation of a larger number of scattering centers within the layer. Thus, a proper concentration of GQDs is crucial for balancing the efficiency of the carrier separation and the transport efficiency.

Table 1 lists the performance data of the reported multicomponent amorphous SBUV photovoltaic photodetectors. From Table 1, the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN SBUV PD fabricated in this study demonstrates an excellent responsivity and EQE in zero-power operation mode. Simultaneously, the device also exhibits a high D* and a large SBUV–Vis rejection ratio. The high performance of the device is attributed to the improvement in the electrical properties of the conductive layer, as well as the passivation effect of conductive layer on the surface defects. Moreover, an additional PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN device (defined as PD5) is constructed by the same method to demonstrate repeatability of high-performance. As shown in Fig. S8 (see Supplemental Information), two PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN devices exhibit similar I-T curves under the same testing conditions and the response speeds of both devices are nearly identical. These results show that it is feasible and reproducible to fabricate high performance ternary amorphous SBUV PV PD by modulating the Schottky barrier height and the interface states at the amorphous compound/electrode interface through interface engineering.

In addition, the application potential of PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD in the field of security communication is explored. Fig. 7 a illustrates the process of compiling, transmitting, and decrypting the SBUV signal. Firstly, the message is compiled as international Morse code and is output as an optic signal by an optical transmitter. Then, the optical signal is received by PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD and converted into an electrical signal. Finally, the electrical signal waveform is displayed by the 4200A-SCS parameter analyzer. Notably, one cycle I-T curve is defined as 'point', and three cycles I-T curves are defined as 'line'. As shown in Fig. 7b, these ordered and stable I-T curves accurately correspond to the text 'GDUT' in the international Morse code. These results verify the potential and reliability of the PP@GQDs/Lu0.39In0.61O/GaN PD in SBUV communication systems.

Han Cai: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Visualization, Resources. Haolin Yang: Writing – review & editing. Weisen Li: Supervision. Jiarong Liang: Formal analysis. Zhuogeng Lin: Data curation. Xingui Tang: Writing – review & editing. Yanghui Liu: Resources. Dan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Wei Zheng: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Open research fund of Songshan Lake Materials Laboratory [grant numbers 2023SLABFK01]; National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 62004229].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.159844.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
